title: Red Wine on Wool
stainChemistry: Red wine anthocyanins (tannin pigments) bond to wool's keratin protein through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions. Wool is delicate: heat above 30°C triggers felting (cross-linking of disulfide bridges in wool protein), and this happens FASTER when wool is wet. Fresh wine penetrates wool deeply because tannins are attracted to protein. We cannot use enzymes (Protein digests wool), strong bleach, or heat. Acetic acid and dilute oxidizers are the only safe options for wool, and dwell times must be minimal to avoid fiber weakening.
whyThisWorks: Cold NSD removes surface anthocyanin without agitation risk. Dilute acetic acid (28% Acetic Acid (diluted)) breaks hydrogen bonds in tannin-wool complex with minimal dwell. Dilute H₂O₂ (not chlorine) oxidizes remaining color. Every step stays cold and brief. The sequence prioritizes wool integrity over complete stain removal—if residual color remains, professional is safer than aggressive home treatment.

spottingProtocol.equipment: White cloth, spray bottle with ice water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Immediately blot from the back with ice water. Press downward—never rub or agitate wool. Most fresh anthocyanin transfers in the first 1-2 minutes. Flip cloth and continue blotting. Work concentric circles from edge inward. Every motion must be vertical pressure only.

Sponge gently onto stain. Press vertically—never rub. NSD suspends remaining anthocyanin. After 2-3 minutes, blot gently downward. Stain should lighten noticeably. This step removes most of the wine color.

Rinse completely in cold water. NSD left behind will interfere with acid treatment. Work from back to front, pressing gently. Water must run absolutely clear with no bubbles. Thorough rinsing is critical.

Now apply acidity for remaining tannin color. Apply in small amounts with dropper or sponge. Press gently. Acetic acid breaks hydrogen bonds in the tannin-wool complex. After exactly 1 minute, blot completely. Do not extend—wool weakens with prolonged acid exposure.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Sponge, white cloth, 3% peroxide diluted 1:1 with cold water
spottingProtocol.instruction: If brown tannin color remains, 5% solution). Sponge gently. Peroxide oxidizes anthocyanin pigment into colorless compounds. Wool is protein—dilute peroxide is safer. After 1-2 minutes maximum, blot completely. Do not oversoak.
Final thorough rinse in cold water. All acid and peroxide must be removed. Residual chemicals will continue reacting and may damage wool. Work from back to front. Water must run clear.

Squeeze gently in towel—never wring. Lay flat on clean dry towel. Reshape while damp if needed. NEVER use heat. Wool must dry slowly and evenly. Hot air triggers felting. Once dry, check for residual color—if faint brown remains, do not retreat. Call professional instead.

homeSolutions: Test on a hidden area first (inside seam or hem) — wool dyes may react to cleaning agents.
FRESH: Ice water blot from back, then NSD soak (NSD cold, 2-3 min).
REMAINING COLOR: Dilute white vinegar (1:1 cold, 1 min only), blot, rinse thoroughly.
BROWN TANNIN: Dilute H₂O₂ (1.5% cold, 1-2 min max), blot, rinse thoroughly cold.
IF STILL REMAINING: Stop. Call professional—risk of damage increases with extended treatment.
materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — wool dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
NEVER use hot water—felting occurs faster when wet + warm
NEVER rub or agitate—causes pilling and structural damage
NEVER use enzymes—protease digests wool's own keratin
NEVER use bleach (chlorine or strong oxidizers)—weakens wool
NEVER use strong alkaline cleaners—damage wool protein structure
escalation: If red wine stain is older than 6-8 hours or if home treatment doesn't fully remove color, STOP and call professional wool specialist. Risk of damage increases exponentially with extended treatment. Professional may use solvent system or inhibitor-formula enzymes designed specifically for wool.

household.name: Wool-Safe Detergent (pH-neutral, enzyme-free)
household.note: Enzyme-free, pH-neutral wool wash. Household substitute for professional NSD. Gentle NSD for protein fiber. 10% in cold water. Essential first step.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: Tannin spotter. Dilute 1:1 with cold water. 1 minute max. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

professional.name: Mulsolite NSD
professional.note: Professional cold NSD for wool protein cleaning.

customerExplanation: Red wine on wool is tricky because wool is delicate protein fiber. We use cold water and NSD for safety—but this means we can't be aggressive. Fresh wine responds well to this gentle approach. Older wine may require professional cleaning because risk of damage increases with extended home treatment. Oxygen bleach (sodium percarbonate) is safer than chlorine on wool and effectively removes tannin discoloration.
commonMistakes: Using warm water—this sets tannin AND triggers felting simultaneously
Rubbing or scrubbing—causes pilling and weakens the fiber
Using enzymes—people assume enzymes help. Protein digests wool's keratin protein.
Extending acid/peroxide dwell times—wool weakens rapidly with chemical exposure
scienceNote: Wool protein (keratin) is held together by disulfide bridges (covalent bonds) and hydrogen bonds. Anthocyanins form hydrogen bonds with these same wool protein sites. Water above 30°C triggers hydrolysis of disulfide bonds, allowing wool to cross-link and felt. This felting is FASTER and more pronounced when the wool is wet—exactly when we're treating the stain. This is why every step must be cold and brief. Acetic acid breaks anthocyanin hydrogen bonds (tannin removal), and peroxide oxidizes the remaining anthocyanin double-bond system (color removal) without targeting wool's protein structure.
callPro: If color remains after NSD + vinegar + dilute peroxide treatment, or if wool shows signs of pilling, stiffness, or texture change, STOP and call professional immediately. Wool is expensive and recovery becomes risky after extended home treatment.
solventNote: Professional: Cold soak in Mulsolite (5-10 min), gentle squeeze-out. Rinse thoroughly cold water. For remaining color, acetic acid spotter (dilute, cold, 1-2 min max). If needed, 1.5% dilute H₂O₂ (cold, max 2 min). Absolutely no heat. No reducing bleach. For aged stains, solvent system (perc or Stoddard) safer than extended aqueous treatment. VDS post-treatment recommended.
